questiontext.text:
Quarter-Wave Transformer Design

A lossless transmission line with characteristic impedance \(Z_0 = {@Z0@}\;\Omega\) feeds a purely resistive load \(Z_L = {@ZL@}\;\Omega\). You are asked to design a quarter-wave transformer to match the line to the load at a design frequency \(f = {@f_GHz@}\;\text{GHz}\).

The quarter-wave transformer section is filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity \(\varepsilon_r = {@eps_r@}\).

The speed of light in vacuum is \(c_0 = 3 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}\).

(a) What is the characteristic impedance of the quarter-wave transformer section?

\(Z_{\text{QWT}} = \) [[input:ans1]] \(\Omega\) [[validation:ans1]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number in Ohms, e.g. 86.6

(b) What is the physical length \(\ell\) of the quarter-wave section (in metres)?

\(\ell = \) [[input:ans2]] m [[validation:ans2]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number in metres, e.g. 0.025 or 2.5e-2

(c) What is the electrical length of the transformer section at the design frequency (in degrees)?

\(\theta = \) [[input:ans3]] ° [[validation:ans3]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number in degrees, e.g. 90

(d) What is the magnitude of the reflection coefficient \(|\Gamma|\) at the input of the transformer at the design frequency?

\(|\Gamma| = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0 or 0.05

Now suppose the operating frequency shifts to \(f' = 1.1 \times f = {@f_new_GHz@}\;\text{GHz}\).

(e) Is the line still matched to the load at the shifted frequency?

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

Syntax hint: Select Yes or No from the dropdown.

(f) What is \(|\Gamma|\) at the input of the transformer at the shifted frequency \(f'\)?

\(|\Gamma| = \) [[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.05

(g) What fraction of the incident power is reflected at the shifted frequency? (i.e., \(|\Gamma|^2\))

\(|\Gamma|^2 = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.003

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Quarter-Wave Transformer Impedance

The characteristic impedance of a quarter-wave transformer that matches \(Z_0\) to \(Z_L\) is:

\[Z_{\text{QWT}} = \sqrt{Z_0 \cdot Z_L} = \sqrt{{@Z0@} \times {@ZL@}} = {@ta1@}\;\Omega\]

Step 2: Physical Length

The phase velocity in the transformer section is:

\[v_p = \frac{c_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{{@eps_r@}}} = {@vp@}\;\text{m/s}\]

The wavelength at the design frequency is \(\lambda = v_p / f\), and a quarter-wave section has length:

\[\ell = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{v_p}{4f} = \frac{{@vp@}}{4 \times {@f_Hz@}} = {@ta2@}\;\text{m}\]

Step 3: Electrical Length

By definition, a quarter-wave transformer has an electrical length of:

\[\theta = \beta \ell = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 90°\]

Step 4: Reflection Coefficient at Design Frequency

At the design frequency, the quarter-wave transformer transforms \(Z_L\) to exactly \(Z_0\) at the input, so the line is perfectly matched:

\[|\Gamma| = 0\]

Step 5: Off-Frequency Behaviour

At \(f' = 1.1 f = {@f_new_GHz@}\;\text{GHz}\), the electrical length is no longer \(90°\):

\[\theta' = 1.1 \times 90° = 99°\]

The transformer no longer provides a perfect match, so the answer to part (e) is No.

Step 6: Reflection at Shifted Frequency

The input impedance of the transformer section at the shifted frequency is:

\[Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{\text{QWT}} \cdot \frac{Z_L + j\,Z_{\text{QWT}}\tan\theta'}{Z_{\text{QWT}} + j\,Z_L\tan\theta'}\]

The reflection coefficient is then:

\[\Gamma = \frac{Z_{\text{in}} - Z_0}{Z_{\text{in}} + Z_0}\]

\[|\Gamma| = {@ta6@}\]

Step 7: Reflected Power Fraction

The fraction of incident power that is reflected is:

\[|\Gamma|^2 = {@ta7@}\]

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)\(Z_{\text{QWT}}\){@ta1@} Ω
(b)\(\ell\){@ta2@} m
(c)\(\theta\){@ta3@}°
(d)\(|\Gamma|\) at design freq{@ta4@}
(e)Still matched?No
(f)\(|\Gamma|\) at shifted freq{@ta6@}
(g)\(|\Gamma|^2\) at shifted freq{@ta7@}

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — QWT Impedance

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Physical Length

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Electrical Length

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — |Γ| at Design Frequency

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Still Matched?

[[feedback:prt5]]

Part (f) — |Γ| at Shifted Frequency

[[feedback:prt6]]

Part (g) — Reflected Power Fraction

[[feedback:prt7]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: Z0={@Z0@}Ω, ZL={@ZL@}Ω, f={@f_GHz@}GHz, εr={@eps_r@} | Z_QWT={@ta1@}Ω, l={@ta2@}m, |Γ|_shifted={@ta6@}

questiondescription.text: Quarter-wave transformer design — compute transformer impedance, physical and electrical length, verify matching at design frequency, and analyse off-frequency performance. Week 13 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The quarter-wave transformer impedance is \(Z_{\text{QWT}} = \sqrt{Z_0 \cdot Z_L} = \sqrt{{@Z0@} \times {@ZL@}} = {@ta1@}\;\Omega\).
Incorrect. The phase velocity is \(v_p = c_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r} = {@vp@}\;\text{m/s}\), and the length is \(\ell = v_p/(4f) = {@ta2@}\;\text{m}\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! A quarter-wave section has electrical length \(\theta = 90°\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. By definition, a quarter-wave transformer has electrical length \(\theta = \beta\ell = \pi/2 = 90°\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! At the design frequency the quarter-wave transformer provides a perfect match, so \(|\Gamma| = 0\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. At the design frequency, the quarter-wave transformer transforms \(Z_L\) to exactly \(Z_0\), giving a perfect match with \(|\Gamma| = 0\).

truefeedback.text: Correct! The quarter-wave transformer only provides a perfect match at the design frequency. At \(f' = 1.1f\), the electrical length is no longer \(90°\), so the match is imperfect.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. A quarter-wave transformer provides a perfect match only at the design frequency. When the frequency shifts by 10%, the electrical length becomes \(99°\) instead of \(90°\), so the match is no longer perfect.

Incorrect. At \(f' = 1.1f\), the electrical length shifts to \(\theta' = 1.1 \times 90° = 99°\). Use the transmission-line input impedance formula with the QWT parameters and compute \(\Gamma\). The correct answer is \(|\Gamma| = {@ta6@}\).
Incorrect. The reflected power fraction is simply \(|\Gamma|^2 = ({@ta6@})^2 = {@ta7@}\).

hint.text: Hint 1 (Concept): A quarter-wave transformer is a transmission-line section whose characteristic impedance is chosen so that the input impedance looking into the section (when terminated by \(Z_L\)) equals \(Z_0\). The key formula is \(Z_{\text{QWT}} = \sqrt{Z_0 \cdot Z_L}\). The physical length is one quarter of the wavelength in the dielectric medium.

Hint 2 (Formulas): The phase velocity in the dielectric is \(v_p = c_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\). The wavelength is \(\lambda = v_p / f\), so the quarter-wave length is \(\ell = \lambda/4 = v_p/(4f)\). At the design frequency, the electrical length is exactly \(90°\) and the reflection coefficient is zero. When the frequency changes, the electrical length scales linearly: \(\theta' = (f'/f) \times 90°\).

Hint 3 (Off-frequency calculation): At the shifted frequency \(f' = 1.1f\), the electrical length is \(\theta' = 99°\). Use the transmission-line input impedance formula:

\[Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{\text{QWT}} \cdot \frac{Z_L + j\,Z_{\text{QWT}}\tan\theta'}{Z_{\text{QWT}} + j\,Z_L\tan\theta'}\]

Then compute \(\Gamma = (Z_{\text{in}} - Z_0)/(Z_{\text{in}} + Z_0)\) and take its magnitude. The reflected power fraction is simply \(|\Gamma|^2\).
